topic: A neighbor starts a renovation project
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rhythmic hammering from across the shared fence was a constant, irritating drumbeat in my otherwise quiet afternoon. I watched from behind my window, my jaw tightening as I traced the outline of the scaffolding with my eyes. Every day, the progress was undeniable: dust motes dancing in the sunbeams, the smell of fresh lumber replacing the usual scent of my own garden. My hands, usually steady when tending my roses, felt clumsy and restless. I started rearranging my own potted herbs, meticulously, almost aggressively, trying to assert some kind of order in my small patch of green that felt increasingly overshadowed by the sheer scale of their undertaking. When I finally ventured out to retrieve the mail, I kept my gaze fixed on the construction site, a tight knot forming low in my stomach.

[story 2]

"That sounds… ambitious," I managed, forcing a light tone into my voice as I approached the property line. My knuckles were white where I gripped the railing of my porch. Across the yard, the noise was deafening, a relentless assault on my peace. I could see the bright yellow paint drying on the newly framed window, a stark contrast to the muted tones of my own house. I walked past my own front door, my steps quickening, and I couldn't help but sigh—a sharp, audible expulsion of air that sounded more like a frustrated hiss. I stood there for a moment too long, just absorbing the sight of their transformation, a sour taste rising in my throat.

[story 3]

I spent an hour staring at the blueprints they’d carelessly left leaning against their garage. The lines and measurements seemed to mock my own stagnant routine. A hot flush crept up my neck. It wasn't just the physical change happening next door; it was the feeling that my own life was somehow receding, becoming dull and irrelevant in comparison to this vibrant, noisy overhaul. I retreated inside, pacing the length of my living room. I picked up a book, but the words swam before my eyes. I kept glancing toward the street, half-expecting to see a glimpse of the workmen, their purposeful movements a sharp, unwelcome mirror to my own inertia.
